title.emphasis: Part III: The Rejection of Artificial Personhood: Denying Rights to a Non-Human Author

section.para: In stark contrast to the expanding rights of the corporate person, the legal status of artificial intelligence remains firmly rooted in the category of property. The 2025 decision in Thaler v. Perlmutter serves as the definitive judicial statement on this matter, rejecting the notion of AI authorship and, by extension, any claim to legal personhood for artificial entities. The court's reasoning, grounded in a deeply human-centric interpretation of the Copyright Act, reveals the fundamental barrier that AI faces in the American legal system: it is not, and cannot be seen as, a proxy for human agency in the same way a corporation can.

section.title:
3.1
Thaler v. Perlmutter **: The Statutory Mandate for a Human Author**
section.para: In Thaler v. Perlmutter, computer scientist Stephen Thaler sought to register a copyright for a visual artwork titled "A Recent Entrance to Paradise." In his application, he listed the author as the "Creativity Machine," an AI system he had developed, and claimed that the work was "autonomously created" without human input.17 The U.S. Copyright Office denied the application based on its long-standing policy that copyright protection extends only to works created by human beings.16

Upon appeal, the D.C. Circuit Court unanimously affirmed the Copyright Office's decision, engaging in a rigorous textual analysis of the Copyright Act of 1976.16 The court's central holding was that the statute, taken as a whole, unambiguously presupposes a human author.16 While the Act does not explicitly define "author," the court found that numerous provisions would be rendered nonsensical if a non-human could qualify. The specific statutory language the court relied on included 47:

listitem.para: Duration of Copyright: The standard copyright term is calculated based on the "life of the author" plus 70 years. The court noted that machines do not have "lives" in any legally or biologically cognizable sense.16

Termination and Inheritance: The Act's provisions for terminating copyright transfers after an author's death refer to the author's "widow or widower," "children," and "grandchildren." The court observed that machines do not have family members or heirs.16
Attributes of Natural Persons: Other provisions refer to an author's "nationality or domicile," concepts that apply only to natural persons.16

Legal Capacity: The Act requires a written and signed instrument for the transfer of copyright ownership. The court reasoned that machines lack the legal capacity to provide an authenticating signature.19

section.para: The court's conclusion was stark and unequivocal: "Machines do not have property, traditional human lifespans, family members, domiciles, nationalities, mentes reae, or signatures".16 Therefore, reading the Copyright Act to require human authorship is the only interpretation that comports with the statute's text, structure, and design. The court deliberately avoided the broader constitutional questions about authorship and declined to address whether Thaler himself could be considered the author by virtue of creating and operating the AI, as Thaler had strategically waived that argument in his filings to force a direct ruling on the AI's status.17

title.emphasis: 3.2 The Legal Status of AI as a "Directed Hand"

section.para: The Thaler ruling solidifies the legal status of AI as a highly sophisticated tool, an instrument wielded by a human, rather than an autonomous agent with its own legal standing. This aligns with the legal theory that "all externally directed intellectual property creation is an act of service".48 When a human uses an AI to generate a work, the human is the one providing the creative service of authorship, and the AI is the "directed hand" or instrument through which that service is performed. The court's refusal to see the "Creativity Machine" as an author reinforces its status as property, not an agent.

This stands in direct contrast to the "work made for hire" doctrine, which is often applied in the corporate context. In a work-for-hire scenario, a human author creates a work, and the copyright ownership is immediately vested in the employer, a corporate person.20 This is a transfer of rights from one legal person (the human) to another (the corporation). In
Thaler, the court's finding was more fundamental: because the author was not human, no copyright ever came into existence in the first place. There was no initial right to be transferred.16

This distinction illuminates the central thesis that reconciles the disparate outcomes in Thaler, Citizens United, and Hobby Lobby. The courts confer rights upon non-human entities only when those entities are perceived as a direct proxy for the exercise of the rights of their human constituents. This "Proxy Principle" is the decisive factor.

listitem.para: In Citizens United, the Supreme Court granted speech rights because it viewed the corporation as an "association of citizens," a vehicle for their collective expression.10 The right was seen as derivative of the members' rights.

In Hobby Lobby, the Court granted a religious exemption because it saw the corporation as a means for its owners to achieve their "desired ends," a conduit for their personal religious exercise.36 The right was derivative of the owners' rights.
In both instances, the legal fiction of the corporation was treated as a transparent vessel through which the Court could see and protect the rights of the human actors behind it.

section.para: In Thaler, the plaintiff made the strategic—and legally fatal—decision to list the "Creativity Machine" as the sole author, explicitly emphasizing its autonomy and the absence of "traditional human authorship".17 He presented the AI not as a proxy for his own creativity, but as an independent creator. Faced with a purely non-human entity claiming a right for itself, the court looked to the human-centric text of the Copyright Act and found no human agent from whom a right could be derived. The "Proxy Principle" failed because there was no human principal to represent. The inescapable conclusion is that in current American jurisprudence, legal personhood and its attendant rights are not granted based on an entity's functional capabilities—such as intelligence, creativity, or autonomy—but on its legally recognized role as a stand-in for human agency and interests.
